_metadata.title: BCI PII Detection Patterns
_metadata.description: Pattern-matching rules for detecting personally identifiable information in BCI data pipelines. Each pattern maps to one or more regulatory frameworks. Uses regex (PCRE-compatible) syntax.

direct_identifiers.label: Direct Identifiers
direct_identifiers.description: Data elements that directly identify a natural person

neural_identifiers.label: Neural Identifiers
neural_identifiers.description: Neural data that could identify or re-identify a person, including brainwave signatures and cognitive biomarkers

quasi_identifiers.label: Quasi-Identifiers
quasi_identifiers.description: Data elements that could re-identify a person when combined with other data

health_identifiers.label: Health/Clinical Identifiers
health_identifiers.description: Protected health information in BCI clinical contexts

consent_gaps.label: Consent Documentation Gaps
consent_gaps.description: Missing or insufficient consent records for neural data processing

retention_violations.label: Data Retention Violations
retention_violations.description: Neural data stored beyond necessary retention periods or without deletion mechanisms

patterns.name: Email address in neural data pipeline
patterns.description: Email address detected in BCI data context. Direct identifier that links neural recordings to a natural person.
patterns.remediation: Replace with anonymized subject ID (e.g., sub-001). Store the mapping in a separate, access-controlled lookup table.

patterns.name: Phone number in neural data pipeline
patterns.description: Phone number detected near BCI data references. Direct identifier.
patterns.remediation: Remove from neural data pipeline. Store in separate identity management system.

patterns.name: Social Security / National ID number
patterns.description: Possible SSN/national ID number near BCI data. Highest-risk direct identifier.
patterns.remediation: Immediately remove. National ID numbers must never appear in neural data pipelines.

patterns.name: Person name in EEG/BCI filename
patterns.description: Neural data filename appears to contain a person's name. The primary pattern checks common test names; the broad pattern catches any capitalized word pair before a neural data extension (e.g., Sarah_Connor_resting.edf). Broad pattern may produce false positives on non-name pairs.

patterns.name: Subject name in EDF/BDF header fields
patterns.description: Neural data file header contains identifiable subject information.
patterns.remediation: Set patient_id to anonymized code. Use pyedflib: f.setPatientName('sub-001')

patterns.name: Date of birth in BCI context
patterns.description: Date of birth near neural data context. Quasi-identifier that enables re-identification.
patterns.remediation: Replace with age range or remove entirely. If clinically necessary, store in separate demographics file with access controls.

patterns.name: IP address in BCI data stream
patterns.description: IP address in BCI data pipeline. Considered personal data under GDPR when it can identify a person.
patterns.remediation: Use hostnames or anonymize IPs in logs. For LSL streams, bind to localhost where possible.

patterns.name: GPS/location coordinates in BCI context
patterns.description: Geolocation data in BCI context. Enables physical identification of recording location/subject.
patterns.remediation: Remove geolocation from neural data metadata. If needed for research, reduce precision to city level.

patterns.name: Device serial number linked to subject
patterns.description: Hardware identifier linked to BCI recordings. Can be used to track subjects across sessions.
patterns.remediation: Hash device identifiers before storing with neural data. Use session-specific pseudonyms.

patterns.name: Neural biometric signature storage
patterns.description: Neural biometric data detected. Brainwave signatures are unique identifiers under neurorights frameworks. Chile's Neurorights Law and UNESCO recommendations specifically protect neural data as biometric data requiring heightened safeguards.
patterns.remediation: Neural biometric templates require: (1) explicit informed consent, (2) encryption at rest and in transit, (3) defined retention period, (4) right to deletion. Consider whether biometric use is proportionate to the purpose.

patterns.name: Cognitive state classification without consent gate
patterns.context_filter: Only flag when pattern appears near BCI/neural context keywords (eeg, neural, bci, brain, brainwave, neuro, cortical, mne, pylsl, brainflow). Do NOT flag in general NLP sentiment analysis, video game analytics, sleep tracking from wearable HR data, or chatbot intent classification contexts.
patterns.description: Cognitive state classification detected in BCI context. Inferring mental states from neural data triggers the highest protection tier under neurorights frameworks. The Mind Act and Chile Neurorights Law specifically protect mental privacy and cognitive liberty. Note: this pattern may match non-BCI uses (sentiment analysis, game analytics). Only flag when BCI/neural context is present.
patterns.remediation: Require explicit consent before cognitive classification. Document: (1) what states are classified, (2) purpose limitation, (3) accuracy limitations, (4) right to opt out. Never present inferred states as ground truth (see G1: Neuromodesty).

patterns.name: Raw neural data export without anonymization
patterns.description: Raw neural data export without apparent anonymization step. Raw EEG contains biometric characteristics that persist across sessions and can re-identify subjects.
patterns.remediation: Apply anonymization before export: (1) remove header PII, (2) strip timing metadata that could correlate with external events, (3) consider adding noise to prevent biometric re-identification if appropriate for the use case.

patterns.name: Clinical diagnosis in BCI metadata
patterns.description: Clinical diagnosis information linked to neural data. Health data paired with neural recordings creates highest-risk data category.
patterns.remediation: Store clinical data separately from neural recordings. Link only via anonymized subject ID. Apply purpose limitation — clinical metadata should only be present in clinical analysis pipelines, not raw data exports.

patterns.name: Missing consent sidecar for neural data
patterns.description: Neural data file detected without corresponding .consent.json sidecar. All neurorights frameworks require documented consent for neural data processing.
patterns.remediation: Create a .consent.json sidecar file containing: (1) consent_type (informed/broad/specific), (2) purpose, (3) date_obtained, (4) data_controller, (5) retention_period, (6) right_to_withdraw documented.

patterns.name: Neural data processing without purpose limitation
patterns.description: Neural data processing without purpose limitation. GDPR, Chile Neurorights, and UNESCO all require that neural data processing be limited to the specific purpose for which consent was obtained.
patterns.remediation: Define and enforce purpose limitation: (1) document specific processing purposes, (2) implement access controls per purpose, (3) audit data access against stated purposes.

patterns.name: Neural data without retention policy
patterns.description: Neural data stored without defined retention period. GDPR storage limitation principle and UNESCO recommendations require that data not be kept longer than necessary.
patterns.remediation: Define retention period based on processing purpose. Implement automated deletion or anonymization at retention expiry. Document retention policy in data processing agreement.

patterns.name: Cross-border neural data transfer
patterns.description: Neural data transfer to cloud/external service detected. Cross-border data transfers of neural data require adequacy decisions or appropriate safeguards under GDPR and additional protections under neurorights frameworks.
patterns.remediation: Verify: (1) receiving jurisdiction has adequate data protection, (2) standard contractual clauses in place, (3) data processing agreement covers neural/biometric data, (4) transfer impact assessment completed.

patterns.name: Neurostimulation parameters without safety bounds
patterns.description: Neurostimulation parameters configured without apparent safety bounds. Physical safety is a prerequisite for all neurorights — the Mind Act and Chile law both protect physical and mental integrity.
patterns.remediation: Implement safety bounds: (1) maximum current density limits per IEC 60601, (2) session duration limits, (3) cumulative dose tracking, (4) emergency shutoff mechanism. Document limits in device configuration.

GDPR.neural_data_classification: Special category data under Art.9(1) when linked to identifiable persons — biometric data for identification, health data
GDPR.bci_relevance: EEG data paired with identity = biometric data. Cognitive state inference = automated profiling. Both trigger Art.9 and Art.22 protections.

CCPA.full_name: California Consumer Privacy Act / CPRA (2023 amendments)
CCPA.neural_data_classification: Personal information under 1798.140(o); biometric information under 1798.140(c); sensitive personal information under 1798.140(ae)
CCPA.bci_relevance: Neural data is biometric information. CPRA amendments (2023) added 'sensitive personal information' category that includes biometrics.

Chile_Neurorights.full_name: Chile Constitutional Amendment on Neurorights + Neurodata Protection Law (2021/2024)
Chile_Neurorights.neural_data_classification: Brain data explicitly classified as organ tissue data requiring the highest protection level
key_articles: Art.2 — mental integrity as constitutional right
Art.3 — neural data as organ tissue (not just personal data)
Art.4 — consent requirements for neural data
Chile_Neurorights.bci_relevance: First legal framework to treat neural data as categorically different from other personal data. Sets precedent for all neurorights legislation.

UNESCO_Recommendation.full_name: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Neurotechnology (2025 draft / 2024 framework)
UNESCO_Recommendation.neural_data_classification: Neural data as requiring specific ethical governance beyond general data protection
key_articles: Art.16 — purpose limitation and data minimization
UNESCO_Recommendation.bci_relevance: Global normative framework. Not legally binding but increasingly referenced in national legislation and corporate governance.

Mind_Act.full_name: MIND Act (proposed US federal legislation — Moreno-inspired Neurodata Integrity and Digital Safety)
Mind_Act.neural_data_classification: Neural data as sensitive health data requiring specific protections
key_sections: Sec.3 — consent requirements for neural data collection
Sec.5 — mental integrity and physical safety requirements
Mind_Act.bci_relevance: Proposed US federal framework. Not yet enacted but signals regulatory direction. References Chile and UNESCO frameworks.
Mind_Act.status: Proposed legislation — not enacted. Referenced here for forward-looking compliance planning.

HIPAA.full_name: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (1996, amended by HITECH 2009)
HIPAA.jurisdiction: United States — covered entities and business associates
HIPAA.neural_data_classification: Protected Health Information (PHI) when neural data is associated with identifiable individuals and held by a covered entity or business associate
HIPAA.bci_relevance: EEG/BCI data in clinical settings (hospitals, clinics, clinical trials) is PHI when linked to identifiable patients. Requires BAAs with cloud providers, minimum necessary data use, and breach notification within 60 days.
